scope: rust-CAN is a software library component used in road-vehicle ECUs. This TARA covers cybersecurity threats arising from rust-CAN's CAN bus interface when integrated into an ECU that communicates on an in-vehicle CAN network. System-level CAL may differ after architectural decomposition. Eavesdropping (T-CAN-06) is risk-accepted at library level — confidentiality is a physical/system concern.

assets.name: CAN frame integrity
assets.description: Correctness of frame IDs, DLC, and payload bytes as seen by the application

assets.name: E2E-protected payload authenticity
assets.description: Guarantee that Receiver.unwrap() payloads originate from a genuine Protector with correct sequence

assets.name: CAN bus availability
assets.description: Continuous, timely delivery of CAN frames to all subscribers; bus not blocked or saturated

assets.name: ISO-TP session availability
assets.description: ISO-TP receive tasks complete within the configured timeout and release the Tokio runtime

assets.name: DBC parser robustness
assets.description: DBC parse() does not panic or leak memory on any input

threatScenarios.title: Frame injection — invalid ID or DLC
threatScenarios.description: An attacker on the CAN bus injects frames with out-of-range IDs or invalid DLC values to corrupt application state or cause unexpected behaviour in downstream consumers.
threatScenarios.attackPath: Attacker gains physical or logical access to the CAN bus; crafts raw frames with id > 0x7FF (standard) or id > 0x1FFFFFFF (extended); sends frames before validate_frame() can gate them.
threatScenarios.residualRisk: Low — validate_frame() is the mandatory gate before any send path

threatScenarios.title: Payload tampering — active man-in-the-middle
threatScenarios.description: An attacker intercepts and modifies payload bytes of an E2E-protected frame. The CRC-16 does not provide keyed authentication; an attacker who observes frames can re-compute a valid CRC for a forged payload.
threatScenarios.attackPath: Attacker sniffs CAN traffic to learn frame structure; modifies payload bytes; re-computes CRC-16 over forged bytes (trivial — no key material); re-injects frame.
threatScenarios.note: REQ-SEC-002 mitigates random errors (safety). REQ-SEC-006 (cryptographic MAC) is required to mitigate adversarial forgery. CRC-16 alone is INSUFFICIENT for security-level integrity against an active adversary.
threatScenarios.residualRisk: Medium (CRC only) → Low (with HMAC per REQ-SEC-006)

threatScenarios.title: Replay attack — re-injection of captured frames
threatScenarios.description: An attacker captures a legitimately protected frame and re-injects it at a later time to replay a past command (e.g., a previously-valid brake-release).
threatScenarios.attackPath: Attacker records frames during normal operation; replays one or more frames at a chosen later time.
threatScenarios.residualRisk: Low — monotonic 32-bit counter rejected by Receiver; wrap at 2^32 documented in .fusa-hara.json H-CAN-03

threatScenarios.title: Bus-off DoS — dominant-bit flooding
threatScenarios.description: An attacker (or faulty node) repeatedly transmits dominant bits to cause CAN error frames, pushing the target ECU's transmit error counter above 255 into bus-off state.
threatScenarios.attackPath: Attacker node sends malformed CAN frames at high rate; CAN controller error counters increment; at TEC > 255 the controller enters bus-off and stops transmitting.
threatScenarios.note: Bus-off recovery is a hardware/kernel responsibility. rust-CAN exposes Error::BusOff so applications can detect and respond. Physical isolation requires system-level controls outside library scope.
threatScenarios.residualRisk: Medium — application must implement recovery policy; rust-CAN provides detection via Error::BusOff

threatScenarios.title: Node impersonation — CAN ID spoofing
threatScenarios.description: CAN has no built-in source authentication. Any node on the bus can transmit frames with any CAN ID. An attacker impersonates a trusted ECU by sending frames with its CAN ID.
threatScenarios.attackPath: Attacker connects a rogue node; transmits frames with a stolen or guessed CAN ID; application receives frames it believes are from a trusted source.
threatScenarios.note: CAN ID validation (REQ-SEC-001) only rejects structurally invalid IDs; it cannot authenticate the source. Mitigation requires a cryptographic MAC keyed per-node (REQ-SEC-006). System-level: HSM-backed key management is out of rust-CAN library scope.
threatScenarios.residualRisk: Medium — fully mitigated only with per-node HMAC keys (REQ-SEC-006 + system key management)

threatScenarios.title: Eavesdropping — passive traffic capture
threatScenarios.description: CAN is a broadcast medium. Any node on the bus can receive all frames. An attacker with physical access can passively capture all traffic.
threatScenarios.attackPath: Attacker attaches a CAN analyser to the bus; captures all frames including safety-critical and diagnostic traffic.

riskAcceptance.rationale: CAN is inherently a broadcast medium with no confidentiality at the physical layer. Encryption on CAN is non-standard and adds significant latency for safety-critical signals. Confidentiality of diagnostic data is a system-level concern (e.g., UDS security access). rust-CAN is a transport library and does not add encryption. Applications with confidentiality requirements SHALL use application-layer encryption outside rust-CAN scope.

threatScenarios.residualRisk: Accepted — physical access assumed for in-vehicle systems; OEM security architecture handles encryption at system level

threatScenarios.title: Frame-flooding DoS — subscriber saturation
threatScenarios.description: An attacker floods the CAN bus with valid frames at a rate exceeding the subscriber's processing capacity, causing buffer overflow and silent frame drops.
threatScenarios.attackPath: Attacker sends frames at bus maximum rate (1 Mbit/s ≈ 10000 standard frames/sec); subscriber queue fills; frames are dropped silently under DropNewest policy.
threatScenarios.residualRisk: Low — per-subscriber rate limit enforced in SubInner::push()

threatScenarios.title: ISO-TP session exhaustion — incomplete multi-frame transfer
threatScenarios.description: An attacker sends an ISO-TP First Frame but withholds Consecutive Frames, causing IsoTpConn.recv() to block until timeout on every call.
threatScenarios.attackPath: Attacker sends FF with large length; holds open the session; ISO-TP receiver waits; multiple parallel sessions exhaust Tokio task resources.
threatScenarios.residualRisk: Low — timeout bounds blocking to 250 ms (default)

threatScenarios.title: DBC parser crash — malformed input
threatScenarios.description: An attacker supplies a malformed DBC file (e.g., via a compromised OTA update or diagnostic session) causing parse() to panic, crash the process, or consume unbounded memory.
threatScenarios.attackPath: Attacker delivers crafted DBC bytes through a diagnostic channel or file-system path; parse() encounters unexpected input and panics.
threatScenarios.residualRisk: Low — parse() returns Err on all malformed inputs; sec-test confirmed

cybersecurityGoals.description: rust-CAN shall reject all CAN frames with structurally invalid IDs or DLC before they reach any subscriber or transport layer.

rust-CAN shall provide mechanisms to detect and reject tampered, replayed, or spoofed protected messages. Safety-level detection (CRC-16) is provided natively; security-level detection (cryptographic MAC) requires application integration of the MessageAuthenticator interface.
rust-CAN shall resist resource-exhaustion and flooding attacks by enforcing per-subscriber rate limits, bounding all blocking operations with timeouts, and exposing bus-off state to the application.
